name: maxvit

members.name: MaxViTConfig
members.signature: MaxViTConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, depths: tuple[int, ...] = (2, 2, 5, 2), dims: tuple[int, ...] = (64, 128, 256, 512), window_size: int = 7, num_heads: int = 32, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, stem_width: int | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, depths: tuple[int, ...] = (2, 2, 5, 2), dims: tuple[int, ...] = (64, 128, 256, 512), window_size: int = 7, num_heads: int = 32, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, stem_width: int | None = None)

members.summary: Configuration dataclass for every MaxViT variant.
members.extended:
`MaxViTConfig` is an immutable container that fully specifies the
architecture of a MaxViT (Tu et al., 2022).  MaxViT combines an
MBConv convolutional stage with two complementary sparse
self-attention mechanisms — *block attention* and *grid attention*
— in every MaxViT block, realising dense local + dense global
self-attention at linear complexity in the number of tokens.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Number of output classes for the classification head.  Defaults
to `1000` (ImageNet-1k).

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of input image channels.  Defaults to `3` (RGB).

parameters.name: depths
parameters.description:
Number of MaxViT blocks in each of the four stages.  Defaults
to `(2, 2, 5, 2)` (MaxViT-Tiny).

parameters.name: dims
parameters.description:
Output channel width of each of the four stages.  Defaults to
`(64, 128, 256, 512)` (MaxViT-Tiny).

parameters.name: window_size
parameters.description:
Side length $P$ of the partition window for block and
grid attention.  Defaults to `7`.

parameters.name: num_heads
parameters.description:
Number of attention heads used by both block and grid
attention.  In practice the reference recipe fixes `head_dim`
to 32 and derives `num_heads = dim / 32` per stage; this
field is informational.  Defaults to `32`.

parameters.name: mlp_ratio
parameters.description:
MLP expansion ratio inside the partition-attention blocks.
Defaults to `4.0`.

parameters.name: stem_width
parameters.description:
Output width of the convolutional stem.  The reference MaxViT
decouples this from `dims[0]`: Tiny/Large keep `stem_width ==
dims[0]` while Small/Base use a narrower `stem_width = 64` and
let the first block's shortcut expand to `dims[0]`.  `None`
(the default) falls back to `dims[0]`.

examples:
Build a MaxViT-Tiny configuration with a 100-class head:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import MaxViTConfig
>>> cfg = MaxViTConfig(num_classes=100)
>>> cfg.depths, cfg.dims, cfg.window_size, cfg.num_classes
((2, 2, 5, 2), (64, 128, 256, 512), 7, 100)

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description: Constant string `"maxvit"` used by the model registry.

members.name: MaxViT

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Return bias tensor of shape (1, num_heads, ws², ws²).

members.extended:
MaxViT combines a convolutional MBConv stage with two complementary
sparse self-attention mechanisms — *block attention* and *grid
attention* — to obtain dense local plus dense global receptive
fields at linear complexity in the number of tokens.  The backbone
is a four-stage pyramid preceded by a two-conv stem, and every
MaxViT block applies (i) MobileNetV3-style MBConv, (ii) block
attention, and (iii) grid attention in sequence.

Given a feature map of shape $(H, W, C)$, *block attention*
partitions it into non-overlapping $P \times P$ windows
(default $P = 7$) and runs full self-attention inside each
window — identical to Swin's W-MSA.  *Grid attention* takes the
same feature map and gathers tokens that share the same intra-window
position across all windows.  Formally, with $H = h \cdot P$,
$W = w \cdot P$:

$$
\mathrm{Block}: (H, W) \to (h \cdot w,\ P \cdot P),\quad
\mathrm{Grid} : (H, W) \to (P \cdot P,\ h \cdot w).
$$

`forward_features` returns the global-average-pooled
$(B, \texttt{dims[-1]})$ feature.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen dataclass specifying `depths`, `dims`,
`window_size`, `num_heads`, `mlp_ratio`, `in_channels`,
and `num_classes`.  See `MaxViTConfig`.

examples:
Build a MaxViT-Tiny backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import MaxViT, MaxViTConfig
>>> model = MaxViT(MaxViTConfig())
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape                       # (B, dims[-1])
(1, 512)
notes:
Reference: Zhengzhong Tu *et al.*, *"MaxViT: Multi-Axis Vision
Transformer"*, ECCV 2022,
[arXiv:2204.01697](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.01697).

attributes.name: stem
attributes.description: Two-layer convolutional stem with stride-2 first conv.

attributes.name: stages
attributes.description: Four MaxViT stages, each containing a stack of MaxViT blocks.

attributes.name: head
attributes.description:
Pre-classification head: LayerNorm → `Linear + Tanh` → final
Linear (also present on the backbone for parameter sharing).

members.name: MaxViTForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)
methods.summary: Return bias tensor of shape (1, num_heads, ws², ws²).

members.summary: MaxViT with a linear classification head (Tu et al., 2022).
members.extended:
Wraps the same multi-axis attention trunk as `MaxViT`
(stem + four MaxViT stages) and adds the reference-recipe head:
global average pool → LayerNorm → `Linear(dim) + Tanh` → final
linear classifier:

$$
\text{logits} = W_{\text{cls}}\,
    \mathrm{Tanh}\!\bigl(W_{\text{pre}}\,
    \mathrm{LN}(\mathrm{GAP}(z^{L}))\bigr) + b_{\text{cls}}.
$$

Pass `labels` to `forward` to compute the cross-entropy
loss in the same pass.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture specification.  Must set `num_classes` to the
desired number of output categories.  See `MaxViTConfig`.

examples:
End-to-end inference with the default MaxViT-Tiny classifier:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import (
...     MaxViTConfig, MaxViTForImageClassification,
... )
>>> model = MaxViTForImageClassification(MaxViTConfig())
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Reference: Zhengzhong Tu *et al.*, *"MaxViT: Multi-Axis Vision
Transformer"*, ECCV 2022.  MaxViT-Tiny / Small / Base / Large /
XLarge reach **83.6 / 84.5 / 84.9 / 85.2 / 85.5 % top-1 on
ImageNet-1k** (Tu et al., 2022, Table 6).

members.name: maxvit_tiny
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *MaxViT-Tiny* configuration:
`depths=(2, 2, 5, 2)`, `dims=(64, 128, 256, 512)`,
`window_size=7`.  Approximately **31M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when
available in the model zoo.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides applied on top of the canonical MaxViT-Tiny
config.  Each override must match a field of `MaxViTConfig`.

returns.description:
A `MaxViT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 512)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import maxvit_tiny
>>> model = maxvit_tiny()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape
(1, 512)
notes:
MaxViT-Tiny reaches **83.6% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6).  See
[arXiv:2204.01697](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.01697).

members.name: maxvit_tiny_cls
members.signature: maxvit_tiny_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: MaxViTTinyWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.extended:
Combines the `maxvit_tiny` backbone with the reference
NormMLP classifier head (LayerNorm → `Linear + Tanh` → final
Linear).  Default output is `num_classes=1000` (ImageNet-1k).
~31M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`MaxViTTinyWeights.IN1K`); a tag
string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with
`weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical MaxViT-Tiny config.

returns.description:
Classifier returning `ImageClassificationOutput` whose
`logits` has shape `(B, num_classes)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import maxvit_tiny_cls
>>> model = maxvit_tiny_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
MaxViT-Tiny reaches **83.6% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6).  Pretrained weights are converted from
timm's `maxvit_tiny_tf_224.in1k` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/maxvit-tiny`.

members.name: maxvit_small
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *MaxViT-Small* configuration: same depths
as MaxViT-Tiny (`depths=(2, 2, 5, 2)`) but wider —
`dims=(96, 192, 384, 768)`.  Approximately **55.8M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical MaxViT-Small config.

returns.description:
A `MaxViT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 768)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import maxvit_small
>>> model = maxvit_small()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 768)
notes:
MaxViT-Small reaches **84.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6).

members.name: maxvit_small_cls
members.signature: maxvit_small_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: MaxViTSmallWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.extended:
Combines the `maxvit_small` backbone (`depths=(2, 2, 5, 2)`,
`dims=(96, 192, 384, 768)`) with the reference NormMLP head.
~68.9M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`MaxViTSmallWeights.IN1K`); a tag
string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with
`weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical MaxViT-Small config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import maxvit_small_cls
>>> model = maxvit_small_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
MaxViT-Small reaches **84.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6).  Pretrained weights are converted from
timm's `maxvit_small_tf_224.in1k` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/maxvit-small`.

members.name: maxvit_base
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *MaxViT-Base* configuration:
`depths=(2, 6, 14, 2)`, `dims=(96, 192, 384, 768)`.
Approximately **96.6M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k or ImageNet-22k pretrained
weights when available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical MaxViT-Base config.

returns.description:
A `MaxViT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 768)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import maxvit_base
>>> model = maxvit_base()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 768)
notes:
MaxViT-Base reaches **84.9% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6).

members.name: maxvit_xlarge
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *MaxViT-XLarge* configuration:
`depths=(2, 6, 14, 2)`, `dims=(192, 384, 768, 1536)`.
Approximately **383.7M parameters** — the largest variant in the
paper.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-22k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical MaxViT-XLarge config.

returns.description:
A `MaxViT` backbone returning a flat $(B, 1536)$
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.maxvit import maxvit_xlarge
>>> model = maxvit_xlarge()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 1536)
notes:
MaxViT-XLarge reaches **85.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6) and **88.5%** when fine-tuned at 512x512
after JFT-300M pretraining (Table 7) — the headline result of the
paper.

members.name: maxvit_xlarge_cls
members.extended:
Combines the `maxvit_xlarge` backbone (`depths=(2, 6, 14, 2)`,
`dims=(192, 384, 768, 1536)`) with the reference NormMLP head.
~383.7M parameters — the largest MaxViT variant.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-22k or JFT-300M pretrained weights
when available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical MaxViT-XLarge config.

examples:

notes:
MaxViT-XLarge reaches **85.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 224x224
(Tu et al., 2022, Table 6) and **88.5%** at 512x512 after JFT-300M

members.name: MaxViTBaseWeights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`maxvit_base_tf_224.in1k` (Tu et al., 2022; ~119.5M params,
84.95% top-1).

members.name: MaxViTLargeWeights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`maxvit_large_tf_224.in1k` (Tu et al., 2022; ~211.8M params,
85.17% top-1).

members.name: MaxViTSmallWeights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`maxvit_small_tf_224.in1k` (Tu et al., 2022; ~68.9M params,
84.45% top-1).

members.name: MaxViTTinyWeights
members.extended:
Single ImageNet-1k checkpoint converted from timm's
`maxvit_tiny_tf_224.in1k` (Tu et al., 2022; ~30.9M params,
83.62% top-1).

summary: MaxViT — Multi-Axis Vision Transformer (Tu et al., 2022).
citation: Tu, Zhengzhong, et al. "MaxViT: Multi-Axis Vision Transformer." Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision, 2022, pp. 459-479.
theory:
MaxViT combines a convolutional MBConv stage with two complementary
sparse self-attention mechanisms — *block attention* and *grid
attention* — to obtain global receptive fields at linear complexity
in the number of tokens.  The backbone is a four-stage pyramid, and
each stage stacks repeats of the MaxViT block consisting of a
MobileNetV3-style MBConv followed by block and then grid attention.

Given a feature map of shape $(H, W, C)$, *block attention*
partitions it into non-overlapping $P \times P$ windows
(default $P = 7$) and runs full self-attention *within* each
window — identical to Swin's W-MSA.  *Grid attention* takes the
same feature map and reshapes it into a *dilated* $P \times P$
grid: tokens that share the same intra-window position across all
windows are gathered into a single attention group.  Formally, if
we tile the spatial dimensions as $H = h \cdot P$,
$W = w \cdot P$, block attention groups along the
$(h, w)$ axis and grid attention groups along the
$(P, P)$ axis:



$$
\mathrm{Block}: (H, W) \to (h \cdot w,\ P \cdot P),\quad
\mathrm{Grid} : (H, W) \to (P \cdot P,\ h \cdot w).
$$

Together the two operations realise dense local plus dense global
self-attention, but each individual attention call has
$\mathcal{O}(P^2)$ cost per token, giving overall
$\mathcal{O}(HW)$ complexity.  Variants Tiny / Small / Base /
Large simply scale stage depths and channel widths along this
shared multi-axis attention design.
